title: Space Robot's Daring Asteroid Landing
story: Zoom! A brave little spacecraft named Hayabusa-2 rocketed through space on a special mission. On February 21, 2019, it did something amazing. The robot ship gently landed on a faraway asteroid called Ryugu. But it wasn't there to stay. Hayabusa-2's job was to grab some rocks from the asteroid's surface and bring them back to Earth for scientists to study.
activity_question: What was Hayabusa-2's mission on the asteroid Ryugu?
follow_up_question: Why do you think scientists want to study rocks from an asteroid?

title: Moon's First Library Takes Flight
story: A cosmic library blasted off for the Moon! The Lunar Library, containing a mind-boggling 30 million pages of digital information, launched on February 21, 2019. This space-age archive rode aboard Israel's Beresheet Lunar Lander, aiming to create Earth's backup on our celestial neighbor. Imagine future astronauts stumbling upon this treasure trove of human knowledge, all stored on special discs designed to last for eons.

title: Ancient Aztec Temple Unearthed by Accident
story: Sparks flew as electrical workers dug into the ground in Mexico City. Suddenly, their tools hit something hard. It wasn't a pipe—it was history! On February 21, 1978, they accidentally uncovered the Aztec Templo Mayor, or High Temple. This incredible find was just two blocks from the city's central square, the Zócalo. Imagine stumbling upon an ancient wonder right in the heart of a bustling modern city!

title: Choo-Choo Revolution: Steam Power on Tracks
story: Puff, puff, chug! A strange new machine rolled down the tracks at a Welsh ironworks. It was February 21, 1804, and the world's first steam locomotive was on the move! Built by clever inventor Richard Trevithick, this iron horse didn't need any real horses to pull it. Instead, it used the power of steam to haul heavy loads along the tramway. This amazing invention would soon change how people and goods traveled, sparking a transportation revolution.
activity_question: What did Richard Trevithick's invention use for power?
follow_up_question: How might steam locomotives have changed the way people lived and worked?

title: Flying Car Takes to the Skies
story: Zoom! A strange vehicle roared down the runway, then lifted into the air. This wasn't just any car - it was the Arrowbile, the world's first working car that could also fly! Invented by Walter Waterman, this amazing machine was tested on February 21, 1937, in Santa Monica, California. The Arrowbile could drive on roads like a normal car, then sprout wings to soar through the clouds. It was a glimpse into a possible future of flying automobiles!
activity_question: What was special about the Arrowbile?
activity_choices: It was the first working car that could also fly
It was the fastest car in the world
It was made entirely of cheese
activity_answer: It was the first working car that could also fly
follow_up_question: Why do you think flying cars haven't become common today?

title: The Longest Life Ever Lived
story: Jeanne Calment entered the world in Arles, France on February 21, 1875. Little did anyone know she'd become a living legend! Calment went on to set an incredible record as the oldest verified person in history. She lived for an astonishing 122 years and 164 days, experiencing nearly three centuries. From horse-drawn carriages to space travel, Jeanne witnessed more changes than most could imagine.

follow_up_question: Can you imagine living long enough to see both the invention of cars and computers?

title: Snap! The Camera That Changed Photography
story: Click! A crowd gasped as Edwin Land unveiled a magical camera in New York City. It was February 21, 1947, and Land had just demonstrated the first instant developing camera. His Land Camera Model 95 could produce a finished photo in about a minute. No more waiting days to see your pictures! This invention revolutionized photography, making it easier and more fun for everyone to capture memories.
activity_question: What was special about the Land Camera Model 95?
activity_choices: It was the first instant developing camera
It was made entirely of glass
It could print photos in color
activity_answer: It was the first instant developing camera

title: Giant Buddha Statue Reaches New Heights
story: A colossal Buddha statue stretches toward the sky in Khatakan Taung, Myanmar. Completed in 2018 on February 21, the Laykyun Sekkya statue stands an incredible 116 meters tall. title: The Slowest Basketball Game Ever?
story: Imagine a basketball game where nobody scores for the entire match! In 1979, on February 21, two Iowa high school girls' teams faced off in what might be the slowest game ever. For four whole quarters, not a single point was made. The suspense grew as the game went into overtime. Finally, in the fourth overtime period, one team managed to score 4 points while the other scored 2, ending this nail-biting defensive showdown.

title: A Starry Home: Eisinga's Amazing Planetarium
story: Eise Eisinga loved the stars so much, he built a planetarium in his living room! Born in 1744 in Dronryp, Dutch Republic, this amateur astronomer created something truly special. His handmade model of the solar system became the oldest working planetarium in the world. Imagine looking up at your ceiling and seeing planets move! Eisinga's passion for space led to an incredible legacy that still inspires people on February 21, centuries later.

title: A Symbol of Peace is Born
story: A simple design changed the world in 1958. British artist Gerald Holtom created a powerful logo for the Nuclear Disarmament movement on February 21. He blended semaphore signals for N and D, forming a circle with downward-pointing lines inside. This clever symbol quickly spread beyond its original purpose, becoming an internationally recognized icon for peace that's still used today.

title: The Never-Ending Basketball Game
story: Squeaking sneakers and swishing nets filled the air as two college teams battled in 1953. Niagara and Siena faced off in what became the longest college basketball game ever played. title: Cherokee Phoenix: A Voice for a Nation
story: Ink flowed and history was made as the Cherokee Phoenix took flight. This groundbreaking newspaper, the first created by American Indians in the United States, rolled off the press on February 21, 1828. Written in both Cherokee and English, it gave voice to Cherokee people and shared their stories with the world.
activity_question: What was special about the Cherokee Phoenix newspaper?
activity_choices: It was the first American Indian newspaper in the US
It was written only in Cherokee
It was printed on phoenix feathers
activity_answer: It was the first American Indian newspaper in the US
bonus_fact: The Cherokee Phoenix used a unique alphabet invented by Sequoyah to write the Cherokee language.

story: Boom! The mountains of Italy shook as Allied forces clashed with German troops. In 1945, after months of tough fighting, victory finally came at Monte Castello. What made this battle extra special? It was the first time Brazilian soldiers joined the fight on land, helping the Allies win this important battle on February 21.

title: Breaking Through: A Crucial WWII Victory
story: Tanks rumbled and soldiers advanced as the US Army's 10th Armored Division launched a daring attack. Their mission? To break through the Orscholz line, a tough German defense. On February 21, 1945, after fierce fighting, the American troops succeeded. This victory weakened Germany's defenses and helped push the Allies closer to ending World War II in Europe.
activity_question: What did the US Army's 10th Armored Division break through?
follow_up_question: Why was breaking through this defense line important for the Allies?

title: Brave Students Fight for Their Language
story: Students in East Pakistan (now Bangladesh) stood up for their right to speak Bengali. On February 21, 1952, they marched to demand that Bengali become an official language. Sadly, police fired on the peaceful protesters. This day is now remembered as Language Movement Day, honoring those who risked everything to preserve their mother tongue.
activity_question: What did the students in East Pakistan demand?
activity_choices: That Bengali become an official language
activity_answer: That Bengali become an official language
follow_up_question: Why do you think language is important to a culture?

title: The First Phone Book: A Single Page
story: Ring, ring! In 1878, finding a phone number got a whole lot easier. George Coy, a telegraph manager in New Haven, Connecticut, created something revolutionary on February 21. He issued the world's first telephone directory - a single page listing just 50 subscribers. This simple sheet of paper marked the beginning of an information era, helping people connect like never before.
activity_question: What was special about the telephone directory George Coy created?
follow_up_question: How many subscribers were listed in this first telephone directory?
